name: VT with Pulse - Unstable
identifyBy: PULSE IS PRESENT - distinguishes from pulseless VT
Haemodynamic instability: hypotension, altered consciousness, chest pain, or acute pulmonary oedema
AV dissociation, fusion beats, or capture beats confirm ventricular origin
management: Confirm pulse present - if absent, treat as cardiac arrest (pulseless VT)
If haemodynamically unstable: synchronised DC cardioversion 100–200J
Ensure adequate sedation/analgesia before cardioversion if time allows
Consider Amiodarone 300mg IV over 20–60 minutes if borderline stability
Prepare defibrillator - may deteriorate to pulseless VT or VF
pearls: 'Stable' VT is a dangerous misnomer - ALL VT is potentially life-threatening
Synchronised cardioversion is preferred over drugs when haemodynamically compromised
Adenosine is contraindicated - can precipitate VF if wrong diagnosis (pre-excited pathway)
Broad complex tachycardia with haemodynamic compromise = treat as VT first
If uncertain: use the Brugada criteria or default to treatment for VT
